shower.thought: The things you're most afraid to say are often the things everyone else is also afraid to say.
If you remember something from your childhood, you're not remembering the event—you're remembering the last time you remembered it.
We spend our whole lives trying to figure out who we are, but we only get good at it right when we're supposed to stop.
Your phone knows more about your actual priorities than you do.
Time moves slower when you're waiting for something because you're actually paying attention, which is ironic because waiting is when we want time to move faster.
Most of your identity is just repeated decisions you've made about yourself that eventually became habits.
You'll never know if you made the right choice because you'll never see what would have happened if you chose differently.
The reason we feel fake sometimes is that we're constantly casting ourselves for different audiences, and one of them might be the real performance.
Every apology you accept contains the unspoken belief that the person won't do it again, which is often just wishful thinking.
We treat our inboxes like our brains—overstuffed with things we'll probably never deal with.
Your sense of humor reveals what you've decided is safe to be vulnerable about.
The saddest thing about getting older isn't losing youth, it's realizing that some people will never change no matter how much you change.
You're nostalgic for things that were actually kind of bad, just because you were different then.
Most people aren't afraid of being alone—they're afraid of being alone with their own thoughts.
The moment you decide someone is fundamentally broken is often the moment you stop actually seeing them.
You can't prove you're a good person; you can only prove you're a good person to this specific audience on this specific day.
Confidence isn't the absence of doubt—it's doubt that decided to show up anyway.
We avoid certain people not because they're bad, but because they hold up a mirror to something we don't want to see about ourselves.
The conversations that matter most usually start by accident and end before you realize they were important.
Your relationship with time changes completely once you realize you're not going to live forever, but you still can't actually feel that change happening.

motivational.thought: Your limitation is not your obstacle. It's your starting point. — Unknown
The quality of your life is determined by the quality of the questions you ask yourself. — Tony Robbins
You don't need permission to become who you're meant to be. — Unknown
Regret is just wisdom's tuition. Pay it once and move forward. — Unknown
The person who says it cannot be done should not interrupt the person doing it. — Chinese Proverb
Every expert was once a beginner who refused to give up. — Unknown
You are not behind. You are exactly where you need to be to become who you're going to be. — Unknown
The obstacle is the way. What stands in the path becomes the path. — Marcus Aurelius
Comparison is the thief of joy, but it's also a thief of your own voice. Stop stealing from yourself. — Unknown
Progress is messy. Growth is not linear. That's not a problem—that's proof you're alive. — Unknown
What you think about yourself is infinitely more important than what anyone else thinks about you. — Unknown
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is today. — Chinese Proverb
You cannot change what happened, but you can absolutely change what happens next. — Unknown
Discipline is remembering what you want more than you want comfort in this moment. — Unknown
The only difference between a dream and a goal is a deadline and a decision. — Unknown
Your capacity to recover is greater than your capacity to break. Trust that. — Unknown
Stop looking for a sign. This is it. — Unknown
The version of you that's one year ahead started making different choices today. — Unknown
Success is not about never failing. It's about failing forward, over and over again. — Unknown
What you practice in private, you perform in public. Choose your private moments wisely. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my girlfriend she was drawing her eyebrows too high.
She looked surprised.

My therapist asked what my biggest fear is.
I said, "Becoming my parents." She said, "That's very common."
I said, "No, I mean literally. They're outside in the waiting room."

I'm reading a book on the history of glue.
Can't put it down.

My doctor said I need to watch my drinking.
So now I drink in front of a mirror.

I asked my boss for a raise.
He said, "You're already overpaid for the amount of work you do."
I said, "Exactly. Imagine what I could accomplish with more money."

People keep telling me I need to live in the moment.
Yesterday I did. It was uncomfortable and I didn't like it.

My friend said he was going to kill himself if his favorite show got cancelled.
It did. He didn't.
I respect the honesty, but also the follow-through would've been appreciated for consistency.
I have a stepladder because my real ladder left when I was just a kid.

The worst part about being ghosted is the false hope.
The second worst part is realizing they were probably right to do it.

My dad told me to stop living in the past.
I said, "I'm trying, but the present isn't going well either."

I used to volunteer at a suicide hotline.
Great benefits, terrible job satisfaction.

My girlfriend asked if I ever think about the future.
I said, "Only the next few seconds, and usually just to figure out what excuse to give you."

I'm not saying my life is meaningless, but my therapist keeps suggesting I get a hobby.
I think she's given up on the bigger stuff.

My dating profile says I'm "emotionally unavailable."
Three people matched thinking it was a challenge.
They were right. They failed.

I told my friend I was going to start journaling to process my emotions.
He said, "That's great!"
I said, "Yeah, I'm calling it my suicide note. Very long though."

My wife asked if I love her.
I said, "Of course."
She said, "You didn't even look up from your phone."
I said, "I've already seen you. Twice. I know what you look like."

I joined a support group for people with low self-esteem.
Apparently I'm not good enough for that either.

My coworker asked what I wanted for my birthday.
I said, "A different life."
He got me a travel mug.
He was so close.

I'm trying to be more empathetic.
So when my friend complained about his problems, I said, "That sounds terrible."
He left. I think I'm still not doing it right.

The hardest part about adulting isn't the bills or the responsibilities.
It's pretending you have a plan when literally everything is improvisation.
